prose:
The man behind the counter, the Waystation Keeper, greeted her with a nod and an outstretched hand. "Welcome to our little hole," he said. "We've been expecting you. We've heard much of Tamsin of the Vale Settlements."

Tamsin smiled and shook the man's hand.

"We were told that you could tell us of the weather in the south," continued the Waystation Keeper. "Perhaps you could tell us something of the road ahead?"

Tamsin knew from experience that the Crumbling Road would grow even worse as it approached the Snowhold Gap. "The weather is fair," she answered. "The pass is choked with ash, though, and I fear that the winter snows will not come thick enough to wash it away. The fumes from the burning mines in the Gap may even have poisoned the air. If the weather holds fair, as it likely will, then the pass will be safe for your passage."

The Waystation Keeper shuddered at the thought.

"The trouble will be with water," Tamsin continued.

"Dusthaven Waystation's well is nearly dry, making water scarce and travel more dangerous. The eastern springs are the only safe source of water that I know. The people are hoarding their supplies and selling for much more than they should."

prose:
Three ragged men, dressed like barbarians but with more of a nomadic flair about them, slipped out of the darkness at the edge of the torchlight and moved in on Sera.

"Surrender the message, dark dog," the leader growled. "It is for the wizard of Balennar Keep."

Sera did not answer. She knew the power of the wizard, and the leader of the bandits, a giant man with a flowing silver beard, only confirmed her suspicions as he drew a sword and moved in. Sera waited for the man to get set for his initial thrust, then dropped to the ground and swept his legs out from under him.

The other two men, recovering from their surprise, closed in on her. Sera darted away. The night-kin knew the strength of these men, and she could not hope to gain any ground against them. She had to get to the side of the canyon, where the rocky walls would slow their attack. Already she was moving too fast for them.

She spun and dropped one, then dived to the side to avoid the thrust of the second. She rolled away into the darkness and started back toward the torchlight. She saw the silver-haired leader remounting his feet and moving in to attack again, and she had to get back into the shadows quickly.

The night-kin's move had sent a small cloud of dust into the air, and now it swirled about the combatants in ghostly swirls.

Rynn passed the remains of the town of Rimeport, the few surviving buildings long abandoned in the face of the encroaching darkness. The sight of the place petrified her. The people had fled, knowing that they would not survive even if they had managed to defeat the monsters.

Its defenses were grim.

"Come, then," Sylvie replied. "What is it that you need of me?"

"The wound," he replied curtly, getting to the point. Sylvie looked at him curiously as he motioned for her to inspect the injury.

"It is severe," he explained. "I fear that I shall not survive the remainder of this campaign. My death would be an impediment to the continuation of the war effort against the orcs and goblins. The math is clear: one life for ten thousand. It is not a decision of sentiment, as you might think it, but of pure logic. I do not ask for your favor, Healer Dunmore; I ask only for your skill. If you will not move this wound to another, the army will fragment and fall into chaos, and without unity, we shall be unable to withstand the onslaught of the goblin king. If the human cities are destroyed, all of the Northland will be lost to the evil of the Orc Kingdoms!"

Sylvie looked at the wound, the same injury that had felled many of her fellow healers. The wound was indeed severe.
